runs.llm_reason: Facing a pot-sized flop bet on Kd7d6d with deep stacks, a call is the lowest-risk legal continue given no hero hand information. The board is wet and dynamic, so raising without knowing a strong made hand or draw would be too speculative, while folding would be too tight against an unknown range on a connected flop. Calling keeps the pot manageable at high SPR and preserves options for later streets.

Facing a pot-sized flop bet on K♦7♦6♦ with deep stacks, continuing range can include made hands, strong draws, and some pair+draw combinations. Without hero hole cards, a default flat keeps the pot controlled and preserves flexibility versus a polarized-sized bet on a wet board. Raising or shoving would be too aggressive absent stronger hand information.

Facing a flop bet on a coordinated K-high, two-tone board, continuing with a flat keeps in dominated/value hands and draws while avoiding overinflating a deep-STACK pot. Without hero hole cards visible, calling is the lowest-risk legal response that preserves range flexibility at very high SPR.

Facing a pot-sized flop bet on 6d7dKd in a deep-SRP heads-up spot, the board is dynamic and favors continuing with a reasonably wide defending range. Without hero cards, calling is the most prudent visible-context response versus an aggressive flop size; raising would be high-variance and all-in is clearly unwarranted this deep.

Facing a pot-sized flop bet on 6d7dKd at very deep SPR, the disciplined default is to continue only with reasonable made hands, strong draws, or solid backdoor equity and avoid inflating the pot without a strong reason. With no hero cards visible, calling is the most flexible legal response versus a single pot bet; raising would be too aggressive by default and folding may be too tight without hand-specific information.
